text:
Peter Badel has recalled the unforgettable reaction when Algerian boxer Imane Khelif entered the ring in her most recent fight amid ongoing controversy surrounding women’s boxing at the Paris 2024 Olympic Games.

News Corp sports reporter Peter Badel has recalled the incredible moment Algerian boxer Imane Khelif was cheered on in her most recent fight despite being at the centre of controversy surrounding women’s boxing at the Paris 2024 Olympic Games.

Khelif stirred debate after her Italian competitor Angela Carini withdrew from their bout after 46 seconds with concerns over the Algerian's physical advantage.

The Algerian fighter was booted from the 2023 Women’s World Boxing Championships after failing the International Boxing Association (IBA)'s gender eligibility test, reportedly over Khelif's elevated testosterone levels.

Nevertheless, the International Olympics Committee cleared Khleif and Taiwan's Lin Yu-ting to fight in the Paris Games despite both women previously failing IBA-sanctioned gender tests.

“It’s been the biggest story of the Olympics so far,” Badel told Matty Johns and James ‘the Missile’ Magnussen.

Johns proceeded to ask Mr Badel how the controversial boxer was received in her recent match up against Hungary’s Luca Anna Hamori.

“What was the reaction of the crowd when the Algerian fighter went into the ring,” Johns asked on his podcast Matty and the Missile.

Mr Badel said he was astounded to hear Khelif receiving applause and cheers when walking to the ring for her fight.

“The irony is Khelif is getting a groundswell of support,” he said.

“I thought there’d be boos for Khelif when she came out to the ring. I think the tide is turning, there are people who feel sorry for her and as she was walking to the ring there were cheers.

“Then her Hungarian opponent came out and there were boos.”

Mr Badel continued to recall the incredible moment after Khelif won and was overcome with an array of emotions.

“After the bout when she (Khelif) won comprehensively, she then punched the canvas and screamed out her name and then burst into tears,” he said.

“It was this remarkable paradox of emotions – it was almost the siege mentality.”

Johns added that it can be easy in these high stake’s competitions for athletes and viewers to lose the “human side”.

“I always bring it back to a common one but it’s a good one: ‘What if that was your child’,” he said.

Since the match and ensuing controversy, Khelif’s Italian opponent has expressed regret in an interview with Italian media.

“All this controversy certainly made me sad and I also felt sorry for my opponent, she had nothing to do with it and like me was only here to fight,” she said.

“It was not intentional, in fact, I apologise to her and to everyone. I was angry, because my Games had already gone up in smoke.

“I have nothing against Khelif and on the contrary, if I happened to meet her again I would give her a hug.”

Khelif wound up beating her Hungarian opponent 5-0 and will go on to win at least a bronze as she advances to the semifinals where she faces off with Thailand’s Janjaem Suwannapheng.
